cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are going deep into a book that has quietly reshaped how thoughtful leaders think about running a creative company.

It is called Creativity, Inc.: Overcoming the Unseen Forces That Stand in the Way of True Inspiration, written by Ed Catmull, the co-founder and long-time president of Pixar Animation Studios, with the writer Amy Wallace.

If you have ever loved a Pixar film, if you have ever felt that little catch in your throat when a toy realizes it is loved, or a rat cooks a perfect dish, or a lonely robot falls for another robot across a dead planet, then you have already felt the result of the ideas in this book.
But here is the thing you need to understand before we go any further.
This is not a book about how to make cartoons.

It is a book about how to build a place where smart, ambitious, creative people can do the best work of their lives, again and again, without the whole thing collapsing under the weight of ego, fear, and its own success.
And that is a problem every single leader faces, whether you run a film studio, a software team, a restaurant, a classroom, or a family business.
So let me hook you with the tension at the center of everything Catmull believes.
Most of us assume that the key to a creative hit is a great idea.
Find the brilliant concept, protect it, execute it, and you win.
Catmull spent his whole career discovering that this is almost completely backwards.
He came to believe, deep in his bones, that ideas are not the scarce resource.
People are.
Getting the right people and the right chemistry between them matters more than getting the right idea.
As he puts it, ideas come from people.
Therefore, people are more important than ideas.
And he sharpens it into a line worth tattooing on the inside of your eyelids.
If you give a good idea to a mediocre team, they will ruin it.
But if you give a mediocre idea to a great team, they will either fix it or throw it away and come up with something better.
Sit with that, because it flips your priorities.
It means your real job is not to be the smartest person in the room with the best pitch.
Your real job is to build and protect the team, and to build a culture where the truth can travel freely.
That is the whole book.
Everything else is Catmull showing you, through decades of real stories, how absurdly hard that turns out to be, and what he learned from getting it wrong.
Let me take you back to the beginning, because Catmull's own journey tells you why he thinks the way he does.
As a boy in Utah in the nineteen fifties, Ed Catmull had two heroes.
One was Walt Disney, whose Sunday television show pulled back the curtain on how animation was made and made a kid believe that drawing could be a life.
The other was Albert Einstein.
Catmull wanted, more than anything, to be a Disney animator.
But he had a quiet, honest reckoning with himself as a teenager.
He looked at his own drawing ability and realized he did not have the raw artistic talent to become one of Disney's famous animators.
So he let the dream go.

He turned toward the thing he was good at, which was math and physics, and he went to the University of Utah, which happened to be, at that exact moment in history, one of the wildest and most fertile places on earth for a brand-new field called computer graphics.
This is where the accident of timing becomes destiny.
At Utah, surrounded by pioneers, Catmull had a realization that would guide the rest of his life.
What if he could not draw a single frame by hand, but could instead teach a computer to make images?
What if the movies of the future were not painted, but computed?
In nineteen seventy-two, as a graduate student, he made a short animated film of his own left hand, digitized point by point.
It was one of the first computer-animated sequences ever created.
And in that moment he set himself a goal that sounded, to almost everyone, completely insane.
He decided he would make the world's first feature-length film animated entirely by computer.
He had no idea it would take him more than twenty years.
Along the way he met the people who would carry the dream with him.
The most important early figure was Alvy Ray Smith, a brilliant, charismatic computer scientist who became Catmull's partner in building the technology and the team.

Together they ended up at a place funded by a wealthy patron of research, and then, in nineteen seventy-nine, the film director George Lucas, riding high on Star Wars, hired Catmull to run a new computer division at Lucasfilm.
Lucas did not want to make computer-animated movies.
He wanted computer tools to help make regular movies, better editing, better sound, better visual effects.
But he had inadvertently gathered, under one roof, a small band of people obsessed with the idea that a computer could tell a whole story.
They spent years developing the technology, the rendering software that would eventually become RenderMan, the hardware, the artistic techniques.
And they waited for the world to catch up to the dream.
Then Lucas, going through a divorce and needing cash, decided to sell the computer division.
And here enters the most famous and most complicated character in Catmull's professional life.
Steve Jobs, freshly and painfully thrown out of Apple, the company he had founded, bought the little group in nineteen eighty-six for five million dollars, and put in another five million to fund it.
They named the new company Pixar.
Now, I want you to hold a clear picture of what Pixar actually was for its first years, because it is one of the great lessons in the book.
It was not a beloved movie studio.

It was a struggling hardware company that sold an expensive imaging computer almost nobody bought, that tried to make money in medical imaging and government contracts and animated commercials, that lost money year after year after year.
Steve Jobs poured in tens of millions of his own dollars, and there were times he was ready to sell it, times he shopped it around, times it looked like the whole thing was a slow, expensive failure.
The only reason the dream survived was a stubborn belief, shared by a few people, that they were building toward something that did not exist yet.

The person who kept the storytelling flame alive during those years was a former Disney animator named John Lasseter, who had been fired from Disney for pushing computer animation too hard, and who found a home at Pixar making short films.
Those shorts, Luxo Jr., the little desk lamp, and Tin Toy, which won an Academy Award, were the proof of concept.
They showed that this technology could carry not just pretty pictures but genuine emotion, character, warmth.
And it was that reputation, slowly built, that finally led Disney to strike a deal for Pixar to produce a full-length computer-animated feature.
That film, of course, became Toy Story.
When it opened in nineteen ninety-five, it was a phenomenon.
It was the first feature film animated entirely by computer, the goal Catmull had set for himself as a graduate student more than two decades earlier.
He had finally done the thing.
And that is where most business books would end, on the triumphant morning after.
But Catmull tells you that the morning after Toy Story was the moment of his real crisis, and it is one of the most honest passages in the book.
He had spent his entire adult life chasing one goal.
Now he had achieved it.
He stood at the top of the mountain and felt, instead of joy, a strange emptiness and a nagging question.
What now?
What was he supposed to do with the rest of his life?
And out of that hollow feeling came the insight that gave him a brand-new purpose, a purpose more interesting than the technical goal had ever been.
He decided that his real work, from then on, would be to understand what makes a creative organization thrive over the long haul.
Not one hit.
Sustained creativity.
He had watched other companies, including the Disney he had worshipped as a boy, produce brilliance and then lose it, calcify, decline.
He wanted to know why.

He wanted to build a studio that could keep making great films decade after decade, and more than that, he wanted to understand the hidden forces that quietly strangle creativity inside even the most talented, well-meaning group of people.
Those unseen forces are the subtitle of the book, and hunting them became the mission of his life.
So let me give you the first and most important truth he uncovered, and it will feel almost offensive the first time you hear it.
Every single Pixar film, the ones you love, the ones that made you cry, started out bad.
Not a little rough.
Bad.
Catmull says it plainly, all of our movies suck at first.
Toy Story, in early versions, had a Woody who was a sarcastic jerk, unlikable, mean to the other toys.
Up began as something almost unrecognizable from the film you know.
Ratatouille, Wall-E, every one of them went through long stretches where they were confused, boring, emotionally flat, or just plain broken.
And this is not a sign of failure.
This is the normal, healthy, unavoidable state of anything genuinely new.
He gives you the metaphor that runs through the whole book.
Early films are ugly babies.
They are awkward and unformed and unattractive, and they need to be nurtured and protected, not judged and abandoned.
The mistake that kills creativity is to look at the ugly baby, the messy early draft, the rough prototype, and conclude that because it is ugly now, it is worthless and always will be.
Your job as a leader is to protect the ugly baby long enough for it to grow into something beautiful.
If you demand that new things be beautiful before they have had a chance to develop, you will kill every new thing before it can live.
Think about what this means for how you lead.

When someone on your team brings you a rough first version of anything, a prototype, a draft, a pitch, a design, the instinct of most managers is to immediately evaluate it against the finished, polished thing they imagine in their head, and to feel disappointment.
Catmull is begging you to fight that instinct.
The gap between ugly baby and beautiful adult is not evidence that the idea is bad or the person is failing.
It is just the distance every creative work must travel.

And that reframe changes everything about the emotional temperature of your team, because it turns the early, fragile, embarrassing stage from a source of shame into an expected and even celebrated part of the process.
Now, if all your movies start out bad and your job is to make them good, you need a mechanism, a reliable way to see clearly what is wrong and to fix it.
And this brings us to the beating heart of the book, the single most valuable practical idea Catmull offers, which he calls the Braintrust.
Let me describe it carefully because the details are what make it work.

When a Pixar film is in development, the director and their team periodically screen the current, unfinished version for a group of the studio's most experienced storytellers, other directors, writers, people who have themselves been through the crucible of making a film.
Everyone watches, and then they sit around a table and talk, with total candor, about what is and is not working in the movie.
They point at the problems.
They say, honestly, this section is boring, I did not believe this character's choice, the ending falls apart, the middle drags.
And they do it without holding back to spare feelings, because they all understand that a mediocre, polite response would be a betrayal.
But here is the part that makes the Braintrust genuinely different, the part that most companies who try to copy it get wrong.
The Braintrust has no authority.
None.
The people in that room cannot order the director to change anything.
They give their honest reactions and their best ideas, and then the director walks away and decides for themselves what to do with the feedback.
There are no mandatory notes.
Compare this to the way notes usually work in Hollywood, where an executive with power over the budget gives the director a list of changes and the director has to comply or lose the job.

Catmull believes that arrangement is poison, because it strips ownership away from the person who actually has to make the thing work, and it turns feedback into a contest of power rather than a search for truth.

By deliberately removing authority from the Braintrust, Pixar keeps the director fully responsible for the film, which keeps the director fully invested, and it means the feedback lands as a gift rather than an order.
Why does that distinction matter so much?
Because the whole thing runs on a fragile fuel, and that fuel is candor.
Catmull is very careful with his words here.
He does not say honesty, he says candor, and he explains the difference.
Honesty carries a moral weight.
To call something honest implies its opposite is dishonest, which makes people defensive and self-protective.
Candor is lighter.
Candor just means being forthright, saying what you actually think, holding nothing back for political reasons.
And candor, he argues, is shockingly rare inside organizations, because a thousand forces conspire to suppress it.
People do not want to look stupid in front of their boss.
They do not want to insult a colleague they will see tomorrow.
They do not want to be the bearer of bad news.
They read the room, sense the mood the leader wants, and quietly shade their real opinion to match.
Every time that happens, the organization loses a little bit of its ability to see reality clearly.
And a company that cannot see clearly cannot fix its ugly babies, and its movies stay ugly, and it fails.
So the Braintrust is fundamentally a machine for protecting candor, and the machine only works because of a few carefully engineered conditions.
The first is that everyone in the room genuinely wants the same thing, which is for the movie to be great.
It is not a competition.
Nobody scores points by tearing the film down.
The empathy is real.

Many of them have sat in the hot seat as the director whose film is being pulled apart, so they know exactly how it feels, and they deliver even harsh assessments with a fundamental warmth and a shared investment in the outcome.
The second condition is that the feedback is about the work, not the person.
Catmull insists on separating the problem from the person.
You do not say, you are a bad director.
You say, this scene is not working, and here is what I felt when I watched it.
When people feel the criticism is aimed at their work rather than their worth, they can actually hear it.
When they feel personally attacked, every defense in the human nervous system slams shut and no learning is possible.
That separation of problem from person is one of the most portable lessons in the entire book, and you can start using it in your next conversation.
There is another subtlety about the Braintrust that I want you to catch, because it protects the director's judgment.

The members are expected to be excellent at diagnosing problems, at saying clearly and specifically what is wrong and why they think it is wrong, but they are discouraged from being prescriptive about the fix.
They can offer suggestions, but the suggestions are just raw material.
The director owns the solution.

Catmull found that people are often very good at identifying that something is broken and far less reliable at knowing how to repair it, and that the person closest to the work, the one who has lived inside it for years, is usually best positioned to find the real fix once the problem has been named clearly.
So the Braintrust's job is to hold up a mirror, to help the director see the movie the way an audience will see it, stripped of all the intentions and hopes that live only in the director's head.
And that is desperately valuable, because when you make something, you lose the ability to see it clearly.
You know what you meant, so you cannot tell whether it actually came across.
You need other eyes.
Now let me name the enemy that all of this is designed to defeat, because Catmull returns to it over and over.
The enemy is fear.
Fear is what kills candor.
In a fearful culture, people do not say what they see.
They do not raise their hand to point out that the emperor has no clothes, that the schedule is impossible, that the beloved project is not working.
They stay quiet to protect themselves, and the quiet is deadly, because problems that go unspoken do not go away, they grow in the dark.
Catmull spent years thinking about how to build a culture where it is safe to speak up, safe to disagree with your boss, safe to deliver bad news, and, crucially, safe to fail.
And that last one, the fear of failure, is where he offers one of his most liberating reframes.
Here is the line, and I want you to really hear it.
Failure is not a necessary evil.
In fact, it is not evil at all.
It is a necessary consequence of doing something new.
Read that again in your mind.
If you are doing something that has never been done, something genuinely creative, then failure is not a risk you might occasionally run into.
It is a guaranteed, built-in part of the process, the direct and unavoidable result of exploring territory nobody has mapped.
You cannot innovate without failing, the same way you cannot learn to ride a bike without falling.
A child who is afraid to fall never learns to ride.
So the goal is not to avoid failure, which is impossible, but to remove the shame and the danger from it, so that people will keep taking the risks that creativity requires.
Catmull talks about decoupling the fear of failure from failure itself.
The failure will happen no matter what.
What you can change is whether people are terrified of it.

And when you make failure safe, when you treat a well-intentioned mistake as tuition rather than as a crime, people become bolder, they experiment more, they find the discoveries that only live on the far side of a lot of dead ends.
His colleague, the director Andrew Stanton, who made Finding Nemo and Wall-E, had a phrase for this that became part of Pixar's DNA.
Fail fast.
Stanton compared it to being a ship's captain.
Wrong decisions made quickly are better than no decisions, because you can course-correct from a wrong turn but you cannot course-correct from paralysis.
He would say, be wrong as fast as you can.
Get the mistakes out early and cheaply, when they cost little, rather than clinging to a fragile hope that you will somehow get everything right on the first try.
Because the alternative to fast failure is not success.
The alternative is slow failure, the kind that hides until it is enormous and expensive and too late to fix.
Catmull also warns you about the seductive trap on the other side of this, the fantasy of preventing errors in the first place.
Managers love the idea of a process so careful, so well-planned, that mistakes simply do not happen.
And he tells you this is an illusion that costs you far more than it saves.
He puts it directly.
The cost of preventing errors is often far greater than the cost of fixing them.

When you build layer upon layer of review and approval and safeguard to make sure nothing ever goes wrong, you do not actually eliminate the errors, because you cannot, but you do smother the speed and the risk-taking and the initiative that creativity depends on.
You end up with a slow, timid, expensive organization that still makes mistakes, just quieter ones that surface later.
It is almost always better to build a culture that catches and fixes errors quickly than to build a bureaucracy that tries, and fails, to prevent them.
This connects to what might be the deepest philosophical thread in the whole book, the one that gives it its subtitle, the unseen forces.
Catmull calls it the Hidden.
His central humility is this.
Your model of reality is always incomplete.
There is always a vast amount going on that you cannot see.
As a leader, you are constantly making decisions based on a partial picture, and the most dangerous thing you can do is forget that the picture is partial and start believing you see the whole board.
The forces that undermine a creative culture are, by their nature, mostly invisible.
You do not see the good idea that a junior person decided not to mention because they were afraid.
You do not see the problem that got smoothed over in a meeting because nobody wanted to contradict the boss.
You do not see the slow drift of a process from servant to master.

These things happen below the waterline, and a wise leader spends enormous energy trying to surface them, trying to illuminate the parts of the organization that are hidden from view, precisely because they know their own vision is limited.
Catmull is deeply suspicious of any leader who is too confident, too sure they understand exactly why things are going well or badly.
That confidence is usually a symptom of blindness.
Let me tell you the story that, more than any other in the book, makes all of this concrete.
It is the story of the near-death of Toy Story 2, and it works on two levels, so pay attention to both.
In the late nineteen nineties, Toy Story 2 was in production, all of it living on Pixar's internal computer servers.
One day, someone, running a routine command, accidentally began deleting the movie.
Files started vanishing.
Whole characters disappeared.
Woody's hat, then Woody, then whole sequences, wiped out in real time as the horrified staff watched.
They pulled the plug on the machine, but by then something like ninety percent of the film had been erased.
And then came the gut punch.
They went to their backup system, the safety net that was supposed to protect against exactly this catastrophe, and discovered that the backups had been failing.
The backup was no good.
Months and months of work by hundreds of people, a film that was going to cost a fortune, essentially gone.
Now here is the twist that people love to retell.
The movie was saved by an accident of human circumstance.

One of the technical directors, a woman named Galyn Susman, had recently had a baby, and to be able to work from home with a newborn, she had arranged to have a copy of the entire film on a computer at her house.
Somebody remembered this.

They drove to her home, wrapped her computer in blankets like it was itself a newborn, placed it gently in a car, drove it back to the studio, and carefully, nervously, copied the film back onto the servers.
The home backup of a working mother saved a hundred-million-dollar movie.
It is a wonderful story, and it always gets told as a story about backups, about redundancy, about never trusting a single point of failure.
But Catmull tells you that reading is shallow, and the real lesson is much more uncomfortable.
Because here is what he wants you to notice.
The technical disaster, the deletion, was terrifying but it was recoverable, and in a sense it was the easy problem.
The deeper crisis, the one that actually mattered, was that Toy Story 2 was not a good movie.
The version that nearly got deleted was a mediocre film that everyone had been telling themselves was fine.

And after the deletion scare, when the team looked hard at what they had, they realized the story did not work, and they made the agonizing decision to essentially throw it out and rebuild it, under brutal time pressure, in about nine months, in a sprint that pushed people to the edge of their health and their marriages, a period that itself became a cautionary tale about the human cost of a crisis.

The point Catmull hammers home is that the crisis you can see, the dramatic server deletion, commanded everyone's attention and adrenaline, while the far more important crisis, the quality of the film itself, had been sitting there in plain sight, unaddressed, because it was easier to ignore.
The unseen problem was more dangerous than the seen one.
And that is the Hidden in action.
Out of that whole ordeal came a principle that guided Pixar ever after, and it is worth carving into stone.
Quality is the best business plan.

When the exhausted team was tempted to just ship the mediocre version of Toy Story 2 and move on, the leadership refused, because they understood that if you let quality slip even once, if you let the audience learn that your name is not a guarantee of excellence, you begin to erode the only thing that actually sustains a creative company over the long run, which is the trust of your audience and the pride of your people.
Quality is not a department, not a checkbox, not something you bolt on at the end.
It is the strategy itself.
Everything else, the schedules, the budgets, the marketing, is downstream of whether the thing you made is genuinely good.
Now I want to turn to a subtler enemy that Catmull spends a lot of time on, because it is the one most likely to sneak up on you personally, and it is the enemy of process worship.
Every organization needs processes, systems, and routines.
They make the work manageable.
But Catmull describes how process has a dark tendency to slowly turn from a tool into a master.
It becomes the beast.
People start following the process because it is the process, forgetting the goal the process was meant to serve.
They confuse the map for the territory.

He tells stories of how, at Pixar, they would build a system to solve a real problem, and then years later find that people were rigidly obeying that system even in situations where it no longer made sense, because the system had acquired a kind of authority of its own.
The measurements become the target.
The form gets filled out because filling out the form is now the job.
And the insidious thing is that this looks like good discipline, like professionalism, so it is very hard to see and even harder to challenge.

The antidote, he says, is to constantly remind yourself and your team that the process is a servant, that the goal is the movie, the product, the actual outcome, and that any process which stops serving that goal should be questioned and changed.
Do not let the beast run the show.
He is equally wary of another beloved corporate ritual, the mission statement.

Pixar, he admits, went through the exercise of crafting a lofty mission statement, and he came to see it as mostly a trap, because the words on the poster are easy and the daily behavior is hard, and the gap between them breeds cynicism.
What matters is not the aspirational sentence framed in the lobby.
What matters is what you actually do when a real decision is on the table, when candor is inconvenient, when protecting quality is expensive, when the safe choice and the right choice diverge.
The culture is not what you say it is.
The culture is what you tolerate and what you reward, day after day, in the small moments.
A beautiful mission statement over a fearful, dishonest culture is worse than no statement at all, because it adds hypocrisy to dysfunction.
Let me talk now about postmortems, because this is a wonderfully practical piece of the book and a place where Catmull is honest about human nature.
After finishing a big project, Pixar would hold a postmortem, a structured look back at what went right and what went wrong.
And Catmull observes that people hate postmortems and resist them, even smart, well-meaning people.
Why?
Because when a project succeeds, people do not want to dig into what went wrong, they want to celebrate and move on, and dredging up problems feels like ingratitude or nitpicking.
And when a project struggles, nobody wants to relive the pain or point fingers.
So the natural human tendency is to skip the honest reckoning entirely, which means the organization never actually learns from its own experience, and repeats the same mistakes on the next project.
Catmull argues you have to fight this resistance deliberately.

He offers tricks, like varying the format so it does not become a stale ritual, and framing the postmortem not as a hunt for blame but as a way to teach the next team, and even asking people to list what they would do the same and what they would do differently, so that it is balanced rather than purely negative.
The underlying principle is that learning does not happen automatically.
Experience only becomes wisdom if you deliberately, and often uncomfortably, reflect on it.
Doing is not the same as learning.
There is a recurring tension that Catmull returns to again and again, and it is a tension you will feel in your own leadership, so let me name it clearly.
It is the tension between the desire for control and the need for creative risk.
Every leader, especially a good, responsible one, wants things to be predictable, on budget, on schedule, safe.
That desire is not wrong.
But taken too far, it strangles the very creativity you are trying to manage, because creativity is inherently unpredictable and risky and messy.
If you optimize for control, you get safety and mediocrity.
If you abandon control entirely, you get chaos and you go bankrupt.

The job of a creative leader is to live permanently in the uncomfortable middle, holding just enough structure to keep the ship afloat while deliberately leaving enough freedom and slack for the genuinely new to emerge.
And there is no formula for exactly where that line sits.
It is a matter of constant judgment, constant adjustment, constant vigilance.
Anyone who tells you they have solved it, that they have found the perfect process that guarantees creativity, is either fooling you or fooling themselves.
Related to this is the constant balancing act between the needs of the individual and the needs of the group.
A creative company is full of talented, passionate, sometimes difficult individuals, each with their own vision and their own ego and their own needs.
And it is also a collective enterprise, where the film only exists because hundreds of people subordinate some of their individual preferences to a shared result.
Catmull is deeply attentive to both sides.
He does not believe in crushing the individual for the sake of the group, because it is the individual spark that produces the magic.
But he also does not believe in indulging individual brilliance at the expense of the collaborative whole, because a studio of prima donnas who cannot work together produces nothing.

Managing that balance, giving people the freedom to shine while binding them into a team that serves something larger than any of them, is one of the central arts of creative leadership, and it never gets solved once and for all.
It has to be re-balanced constantly, person by person, project by project.
I promised you Steve Jobs, and I want to give him his due, because his arc in the book is one of its quiet pleasures.
The Steve Jobs who bought Pixar in nineteen eighty-six was young, brilliant, impatient, and often harsh, a man who could be brutal in a meeting and who did not always know how to listen.
And Catmull is candid that the Jobs of those early years was not easy.
But the Jobs who emerges over the twenty-five years of their partnership is a man who genuinely grew, who learned empathy, who learned patience, who learned when to push and when to step back.
Crucially, Jobs understood the creative process well enough to know that he should not impose his own taste on the films.
He was fierce in negotiations, a ferocious advocate for Pixar in the world, but inside the building, on the movies themselves, he largely trusted Catmull and Lasseter and the directors.
He protected the studio without micromanaging its art.
Catmull writes about their relationship with real love, and the book was published not long after Jobs died in twenty eleven, so there is an elegiac quality to it.
The lesson Catmull draws is partly about Jobs and partly about all of us.
People can change.
Even a titanic personality can grow in wisdom and generosity over time, if they stay open to it.

And a great partnership is not two people who never disagree, but two people who trust each other's fundamental commitment to the same goal, and who let each other own the domains they are best suited to lead.
Now let me bring in one more big practical idea, because it shows Catmull living his own principles even after decades of success, and it guards against the most dangerous force of all.
In twenty thirteen, Pixar held something they called Notes Day.
Catmull and the leadership had noticed that the studio, now large and successful, was starting to show signs of the very calcification he had spent his life fearing.
Costs were creeping up, and more worryingly, some of the old candor and openness seemed to be fading as the company grew and people became more cautious about their jobs.
So they did something radical.

They shut down the entire studio for a full day, canceled all normal work, and invited every single employee, from the top directors to the newest assistants, to participate in structured sessions to identify the studio's problems and propose solutions.
They asked the whole company, essentially, to give notes on itself, the way the Braintrust gives notes on a film.

It was a deliberate act of institutional humility, an admission from the leadership that they did not have all the answers and that the people doing the work often saw problems the executives could not.

Notes Day surfaced a flood of honest observations and ideas, some of which the company acted on, and, just as importantly, it re-established the norm that candor was not only allowed but demanded, from everyone, at every level.
And this points to the theme that closes the book and that I most want to leave ringing in your ears, because it is the trap that catches the most successful among us.
Success hides problems.
When you are winning, when the films are hits and the money is flowing and the world is applauding, it becomes almost impossible to see what is starting to go wrong inside your own house.

Success creates a powerful pressure to conclude that whatever you are doing must be right, and to stop questioning, to stop looking for the ugly babies and the hidden fears and the process beasts and the fading candor.
The very achievement you worked so hard for becomes a fog that blinds you to the early signs of decline.
Catmull watched this happen to great companies, including the Disney of his childhood, which fell into a long creative slump before its later revival.
And he became convinced that the only defense is relentless, permanent, deliberate vigilance.
You have to keep actively hunting for the problems that success is hiding from you.
You have to keep inviting candor even when everything seems fine, especially when everything seems fine.
You have to assume, always, that there are unseen forces working against you, and that your picture of your own organization is incomplete.
The work of protecting a creative culture is never finished.
It is a discipline you practice forever, or you lose it.
So let me pull the whole thing together before I send you into your week.
Catmull's argument, stripped to its core, is this.
Creativity is not about protecting brilliant ideas.
It is about building and protecting brilliant people and the trust between them.
Because ideas come from people, the people and the culture matter more than any single idea.
Every new thing starts out as an ugly baby, and your job is to nurture it, not judge it.
To turn ugly babies into beautiful films you need candor, the free flow of truth through your organization, and candor is fragile, constantly threatened by fear and ego and politics.
You protect candor with mechanisms like the Braintrust, feedback given with empathy and without authority, where you separate the problem from the person and let the maker own the solution.
You make it safe to fail, because failure is not an evil to be avoided but the necessary consequence of doing anything new, and you fail fast and cheap rather than slow and expensive.
You stay humble about the Hidden, the vast reality you cannot see, and you spend your energy trying to surface it.
You refuse to let quality slip, because quality is the best business plan.
You keep process a servant and never a master.
You reflect honestly on your work through postmortems even when it is uncomfortable.
And above all, you stay vigilant forever, because success will always try to hide your problems from you.
Now here is your concrete, motivating action list for this week, because ideas you do not act on are just entertainment.
First, find an ugly baby and protect it.

Look at a piece of work on your team that is rough and early and easy to dismiss, a half-formed prototype, a messy first draft, a junior person's clumsy attempt, and instead of judging it against the finished thing in your head, ask what it needs to grow.
Say out loud to that person that early and rough is exactly where it is supposed to be right now.
Second, run a tiny Braintrust.
Take one project that is stuck and gather three or four people you trust, and set two rules before you start.
The feedback is about the work, not the person, and nobody in the room has the authority to command a change, they can only offer honest reactions and ideas, and the owner decides.
Then shut up and listen, especially if the stuck project is yours.
Third, hunt for the fear in your culture.
This week, in one meeting, watch for the moment where someone hesitates, softens their real opinion, or goes quiet after a glance at you.
Afterward, go to that person privately and ask what they actually thought, and then, this is the hard part, thank them for telling you the uncomfortable thing, visibly, so everyone learns it is safe.
Fourth, decouple failure from fear on one real decision.
Pick something you have been avoiding because you might get it wrong, and choose to be wrong fast.
Make the smaller, cheaper version of the bet now, get the mistake out early while it is still cheap, and treat whatever you learn as tuition, not as shame.
Fifth, find one process that has become a beast.

Look for a routine your team follows that nobody can quite explain the reason for anymore, the report nobody reads, the meeting that exists because it has always existed, the approval step that no longer serves its purpose, and either fix it, kill it, or make everyone re-articulate the goal it is supposed to serve.
And sixth, hold a two-question postmortem on your last finished project.
Ask your team just two things.
What would we do the same, and what would we do differently.
Write the answers down where the next project can find them, and resist every urge to skip this because things went fine.
Learning does not happen on its own.
You have to make it happen.
And let me leave you with the deepest and most humbling idea in the entire book, the one to carry with you long after you have forgotten the rest.
You cannot see everything.
Your model of your team, your product, your own leadership, is always incomplete, and there are always unseen forces standing between you and true inspiration.
The great leaders are not the ones who convince themselves they have finally figured it out.

They are the ones who stay curious and humble and vigilant forever, who keep inviting the truth to come find them, who protect the fragile new things and the fragile honest voices, and who understand, in their bones, that building a place where creativity can thrive is not a problem you solve once, but a garden you tend for the rest of your life.
That is what Ed Catmull learned across forty years and a dozen beloved films, and that is the gift he is handing to you.
Now go tend your garden.
Protect your ugly babies.
Invite the candor.
Make it safe to fail.
And keep looking, always, for the things you cannot yet see.
Thank you for listening, and go do the work.
